EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large brush fire on the 27th forced the closure of Ferry Road near the terminal of the Cape May-Lewes Ferry in West Cape May. Little if any rain fell during the preceding week in Cape May County.

A large brush fire occurred in Cheesequake State Park in Old Bridge Township (Middlesex County) on the morning of the 18th. The fire caused the closure of the southbound lane of the Garden State Parkway near Mile Marker number 122 for about an hour and a half. Four fire companies help battled the blaze. Approximately five acres of brush were consumed before the fire was extinguished.  The wildfire occurred on the sixth day of relatively dry weather and was believed to have been caused by a tossed cigarette.

A series of low pressure systems combined with a high pressure system passing across nearby Canada to bring a winter storm of snow, sleet and freezing rain from the morning into the overnight on the 14th. Snowfall averaged 4 to 7 inches in northwest New Jersey, 3 to 6 inches in the Raritan Basin and Monmouth County and around an inch in southwest New Jersey.  Ice accumulations averaged one-quarter of an inch in northwest and west central New Jersey and around one-tenth of an inch in southwest New Jersey.  The combination caused extremely difficult traveling conditions as well as isolated power outages. It also was the second consecutive weekend that a winter storm affected New Jersey.  Numerous spin-outs and accidents were reported, although most did not involve serious injuries. In Hunterdon County, a 21-year-old driver and passenger were injured after striking a tree in Delaware Township.  Other tree crashes occurred in Middletown (Monmouth County) and Bernards Township (Somerset County). ||Precipitation types varied location by location with the most snow in Sussex County and little if any wintry weather in southeast New Jersey. In Sussex County, snow began early on the 14thand fell at a steady pace. The snow did not change to sleet and freezing rain until near the end of the event early on the 15th. Across the rest of northwest New Jersey and the Raritan River Basin, the snow began between 6 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST and fell at its heaviest during the early evening. The snow changed briefly to sleet and then freezing rain during the second half of the evening on the 14th. Some lower elevation locations changed over to rain early on the 15th and precipitation ended between 3 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the 15th.  In Mercer and Monmouth Counties and southwest New Jersey, snow began during the afternoon of the 14th and changed over briefly to sleet and then to freezing rain around 6 p.m. EST. The freezing rain then changed to plain rain during the early to mid evening and continued until precipitation ended overnight.||Representative snowfall included 7.0 inches in Lake Hopatcong (Morris County), Blairstown and Belvidere in Warren County and Wantage (Sussex County), 6.5 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County), 6.4 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County), 6.0 inches in Allamuchy (Warren County), 5.8 inches in Whitehouse Station (Hunterdon County), 5.5 inches in Hanover Township (Morris County) and Hardyston (Sussex County), 5.3 inches in Kingwood Township (Hunterdon County), 5.1 inches in Lincroft (Monmouth County) and Woodbridge (Middlesex County), 4.7 inches in Red Bank (Monmouth County), 4.5 inches in Perth Amboy (Middlesex County), Hazlet (Monmouth County), Parsippany (Morris County), Bound Brook (Somerset County) and Stewartsville (Warren County), 3.9 inches in Hillsborough (Somerset County), 3.8 inches in Colts Neck (Monmouth County) and Somerville (Somerset County), 3.5 inches in Clinton (Hunterdon County), 3.4 inches in Lawrence Township (Mercer County), 3.0 inches in Belle Mead (Somerset County), 2.4 inches in Ewing Township (Mercer County), 1.8 inches in Florence (Burlington County), 1.7 inches in Long Branch (Monmouth County), 1.1 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County) and 0.3 inches in Somerdale (Camden County) and National Park (Camden County).  ||Representative ice accumulations included one quarter of an inch in Ringwood (Hunterdon County) and Lake Hopatcong (Morris County), two tenths of an inch in Haddon Heights (Camden County), Trenton (Mercer County), Hillsborough (Somerset County) and Stewartsville (Warren County) and one tenth of an inch in Mount Laurel (Burlington County) and Ringoes (Hunterdon County). ||The winter storm was caused by a series of  low pressure systems, one emerging from the central Rockies and the second forming in the western Gulf of Mexico on the evening of the 13th. They reached Missouri and Mississippi respectively on the morning of the 14th. Meanwhile, a high pressure system with a fresh supply of cold air reached southern Quebec Province, Canada.  While the southern low pressure system weakened, the northern one reached the central Ohio Valley at 4 p.m. EST on the 14th as the center of the high pressure system started to cross the Saint Lawrence River Valley and slowly eased east during the evening and overnight. A new low pressure system was starting to form on the warm frontal boundary in North Carolina. This latter low pressure system started to intensify as the Ohio Valley low pressure system started to weaken. At 10 p.m. EST on the 14th, the former low pressure system reached the lower Chesapeake Bay. This low became the main low pressure system and passed just east of Atlantic City, New Jersey at 1 a.m. EST on the 15th and eastern Long Island at 7 a.m. EST on the 15th. The sequence of secondary formation helped to lock in colder air near the surface in most of northwest New Jersey.

Bands of thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding in western parts of Salem and Gloucester Counties during the early evening on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller creeks as well as poor drainage flooding in Salem County affecting Carneys Point and Oldman Township to the west of the New Jersey Turnpike. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around two inches, most of which fell within one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger strong to isolated severe thunderstorms in southern New Jersey during the evening of the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Pittsgrove Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snow showers brought around an inch of snow during the evening of the 24th to sections of Warren, Hunterdon and Mercer Counties. Temperatures were below freezing and since most iof the snow fell within an hour, locally slippery traveling conditions occurred on untreated roadways. Representative snowfall included 1.0 inches in Hopewell Township (Mercer County) and 0.9 inches in Holland (Hunterdon County) and Greenwich (Warren County) Townships.

The strong northwest winds, low humidity levels and unseasonably dry November helped rapidly spread wildfires in New Jersey on the weekend of the 23rd and 24th. The largest wildfire occurred in Old Bridge (Middlesex County) and consumed about 230 acres near the Cheesequake State Park. The fire was first reported about 8 a.m. EST on the 24th east of Dock Road. The fire spread toward the Central Park Development toward Washington Avenue and Michelle Avenue. Several residents were evacuated. Morristown Road and New Jersey State Route 34 were closed. Cheesequake State Park was also closed as a precaution. Over 70 apparatuses were used to fight the fire. The fire was contained at 330 p.m. EST on the 24th and residents were permitted to return. New Jersey State Route 34 was re-opened later that day.||The second largest wildfire occurred in Port Monmouth north of Pew Creek in Monmouth County on the evening of the 23rd. The fire was reported at 735 p.m. EST on the evening of the 23rd. The wildfire burned woods and weeds between William Street, Port Monmouth Road and Bray Avenue. Residents on Port Monmouth Road and William Street were asked to evacuate as their homes were threatened.  Seventy-five firefighters helped battle the blaze. The fire was declared under control at 910 p.m. EST on the 23rd and firefighters left at 11 p.m. EST that evening. The siding of one home was damaged by the heat, but no injuries were reported.||During the early morning of the 24th, in Franklin Township (Gloucester County) on Tuckahoe Road, another wildfire consumed about an acre of forest as well as a two car garage, a shed and a boat. The fire was declared under control in about an hour. No injuries were reported. Other smaller wildfires occurred in East Windsor Township in Mercer County, Edison and Old Bridge in Middlesex County and Middletown and Howell in Monmouth County.||Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and helped rapidly spread the wildfires. November was also the third consecutive month of below average precipitation in New Jersey.

A strong northwest flow following a cold frontal passage produced wind gusts as high as around 45 mph inland and around 50 to 55 mph along the coast of New Jersey from the evening of the 23rd into the afternoon of the 24th. The strong winds came in two spurts, one just following the cold frontal passage on the 23rd and a more prolonged and sustained period from the pre-dawn hours into the middle of the afternoon on the 24th. Winds started diminishing as a strong high pressure system came closer to the area. The strong winds knocked down weak trees limbs and power lines and also hampered firefighter efforts extinguishing wildfires. The strong winds also greatly hampered firefighters battling a house fire in Sussex County. Peak wind gusts included 56 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 55 mph in Perth Amboy (Middlesex County), 54 mph in West Cape May and Cape May Harbor (Cape May County),  52 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 49 mph in Tuckerton (Ocean County), 48 mph at the McGuire Air Force Base (Burlington County), 47 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 46 mph at Harvey Cedars (Ocean County) and Sea Girt (Monmouth County) and 45 mph in Mullica (Atlantic County) and the Atlantic City International Airport.

Peak wind gusts included 56 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 55 mph in Perth Amboy (Middlesex County), 54 mph in West Cape May and Cape May Harbor (Cape May County),  52 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 49 mph in Tuckerton (Ocean County), 48 mph at the McGuire Air Force Base (Burlington County), 47 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 46 mph at Harvey Cedars (Ocean County) and Sea Girt (Monmouth County) and 45 mph in Mullica (Atlantic County) and the Atlantic City International Airport.

A low pressure system emerging from the Gulf of Mexico combining with a cold air mass in place produced a wintry mix of precipitation at the onset of the event across northwest New Jersey on the 26th. The most protracted wintry precipitation occurred in Sussex County where less than an inch of snow was followed by up to a couple of tenths of an inch of ice in the higher terrain.  South of Sussex County, most of the wintry precipitation was in the form of pockets of freezing rain.  Precipitation started as snow during the morning of the 26th, but transitioned over to sleet and then freezing rain or rain by the afternoon of the 26th.  Temperatures hovered right around the freezing mark, so freezing rain was elevation dependent. Enough warm air moved in during the evening of the 26th to change the precipitation over to plain rain.  Until the changeover, untreated roadways were hazardous. In Sussex County, a tractor-trailer rolled onto its side outside the entrance of High Point State Park on New Jersey State Route 23. The roadway was closed for less than an our until the accident was cleared.  No injuries were reported. ||Snowfall included 0.6 inches in High Point (Sussex County) and 0.3 inches in Wantage (Sussex County).  Ice accumulations included 0.15 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County).  The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure system that emerged from the Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 26th. The low tracked northeastward through the Florida Panhandle on the afternoon of the 26th and into the Carolinas on the evening of the 26th.  Enough cold air was initially in place to cause the wintry mix until the circulation around the low brought in sufficient warm air near the surface from the Atlantic Ocean.

Strong southerly winds occurred during the early morning across parts of southeast New Jersey as a low pressure system moved through the state. Peak wind gusts reached 45 to 50 mph in Atlantic and Cape May Counties and knocked down weak tree limbs and wires.  Atlantic City Electric reported 2,200 of its customers lost power in the southeast part of the state with the outages concentrated along the Absecon and Galloway Township border in Atlantic County. Peak wind gusts included 49 mph in Cape May (Cape May County) and 47 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport.

A band of snow showers brought around an inch of snow during the evening of the 24th to sections of Warren, Hunterdon and Mercer Counties. Temperatures were below freezing and since most iof the snow fell within an hour, locally slippery traveling conditions occurred on untreated roadways. Representative snowfall included 1.0 inches in Hopewell Township (Mercer County) and 0.9 inches in Holland (Hunterdon County) and Greenwich (Warren County) Townships.

A southeastern United States low pressure system that formed on a frontal boundary helped produce 1 to 4 inches of snow across most of New Jersey during the daytime hours on the 10th. The heaviest snow fell across central New Jersey.  The snow caused some school districts to either cancel classes or have delayed openings in the central and northern parts of the state.  Precipitation started as snow across the state between 7 a.m. and 8 a.m. EST, except in the southeast part of the state where it started as rain. The rain changed to snow in those areas by Noon EST, but had difficulty accumulating. The snow fell at its heaviest elsewhere in the state during the morning. It proceeded to move east and reached the North Carolina coastal waters at 7 p.m. EST that evening and moved farther east offshore overnight.

Strong south winds affected southeast New Jersey during the afternoon and early evening of the 29th as an intensifying low pressure system passed across the southeast part of the state and that region was in the warm sector of the system  Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph and combined with the rain, knocked down weak tree limbs and wires. Peak wind gusts included 51 mph in Seaside Park (Ocean County), 47 mph in Cape May (Cape May County) and Island Heights (Ocean County), 44 mph in Tuckerton (Ocean County) and 41 mph at the Marina in Atlantic City (Atlantic County).

A low pressure system riding along a cold front prolonged precipitation long enough that it changed to freezing rain before ending over the higher terrain of Sussex County overnight on the 6th.  Precipitation started as rain during the afternoon of the 6th. As colder air started working its way into the region, the rain started transitioning to freezing rain and sleet late in the evening of the 6th. This occurred mainly over the higher terrain of Sussex County. The wintry mix ended early on the 7th. The combination of sleet and freezing rain accumulated less than one quarter of an inch. Regardless, untreated roadways and walkways were hazardous.

A low pressure system emerging from the Gulf of Mexico combining with a cold air mass in place produced a wintry mix of precipitation at the onset of the event across northwest New Jersey on the 26th. The most protracted wintry precipitation occurred in Sussex County where less than an inch of snow was followed by up to a couple of tenths of an inch of ice in the higher terrain.  South of Sussex County, most of the wintry precipitation was in the form of pockets of freezing rain.  Precipitation started as snow during the morning of the 26th, but transitioned over to sleet and then freezing rain or rain by the afternoon of the 26th.  Temperatures hovered right around the freezing mark, so freezing rain was elevation dependent. Enough warm air moved in during the evening of the 26th to change the precipitation over to plain rain.  Until the changeover, untreated roadways were hazardous. In Sussex County, a tractor-trailer rolled onto its side outside the entrance of High Point State Park on New Jersey State Route 23. The roadway was closed for less than an our until the accident was cleared.  No injuries were reported. ||Snowfall included 0.6 inches in High Point (Sussex County) and 0.3 inches in Wantage (Sussex County).  Ice accumulations included 0.15 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County).  The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure system that emerged from the Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 26th. The low tracked northeastward through the Florida Panhandle on the afternoon of the 26th and into the Carolinas on the evening of the 26th.  Enough cold air was initially in place to cause the wintry mix until the circulation around the low brought in sufficient warm air near the surface from the Atlantic Ocean.

A quick moving Alberta Clipper type low pressure system brought a wintry mix of precipitation to central New Jersey and periods of snow to northwest New Jersey.  Snowfall averaged 1 to 3 inches in the Raritan Basin and Northwest New Jersey and around an inch in the rest of central New Jersey. Ice accumulations in central New Jersey were less than five hundredths of an inch. Untreated roadways and walkways became slippery. Traffic accidents along major roadways were concentrated in and around Mercer County. Speed limits were reduced on the New Jersey Turnpike and Garden State Parkway. Schools in Sussex County were either closed or had delayed openings. New Jersey Transit cross-honored commuting tickets. ||In central New Jersey, precipitation started as snow around 4 a.m. EST on the 17th. A precipitation break occurred between 7 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST. When precipitation started again, it was a wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain. Temperatures rose above freezing during the early afternoon and the wintry mix changed over to plain rain. Then during the late afternoon, precipitation changed back to snow before it ended that day. Across Northwest New Jersey and the Raritan Basin, snow began between 5 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST and fell at its steadiest across the far northwest. Some freezing rain mixed in at times in the Raritan Basin. The snow fell at its heaviest during the late afternoon in northwest New Jersey just as it was ending.  ||Representative snowfall included 3.0 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County) and Marcella and Butler in Morris County, 2.5 inches in Wantage (Sussex County) and Knowlton (Warren County) 2.0 inches in Morris Plains (Morris County), Hope (Warren County) and Newton (Sussex County), 1.8 inches in Bound Brook (Somerset County) and Stewartsville (Warren County) 1.5 inches in Whitehouse Station (Hunterdon County), Belle Mead (Somerset County) and Edison Middlesex County), 1.2 inches in Hamilton Township (Mercer County) and 0.7 inches in Florence (Burlington County) and Freehold (Monmouth County).||The Alberta Clipper type low which caused the wintry precipitation moved eastnortheast from the lower Ohio Valley on the evening of the 16th and reached Lake Erie at 7 a.m. EST on the 17th. From there, a secondary low pressure system formed around Philadelphia and moved off the central New Jersey coast during the middle of the afternoon on the 17th. This low became the primary low pressure system as it moved toward Long Island, New York around the time precipitation ended later that afternoon.

A strong northwest flow following a cold frontal passage produced wind gusts as high as around 45 mph inland and around 50 to 55 mph along the coast of New Jersey from the evening of the 23rd into the afternoon of the 24th. The strong winds came in two spurts, one just following the cold frontal passage on the 23rd and a more prolonged and sustained period from the pre-dawn hours into the middle of the afternoon on the 24th. Winds started diminishing as a strong high pressure system came closer to the area. The strong winds knocked down weak trees limbs and power lines and also hampered firefighter efforts extinguishing wildfires. The strong winds also greatly hampered firefighters battling a house fire in Sussex County. Peak wind gusts included 56 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 55 mph in Perth Amboy (Middlesex County), 54 mph in West Cape May and Cape May Harbor (Cape May County),  52 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 49 mph in Tuckerton (Ocean County), 48 mph at the McGuire Air Force Base (Burlington County), 47 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 46 mph at Harvey Cedars (Ocean County) and Sea Girt (Monmouth County) and 45 mph in Mullica (Atlantic County) and the Atlantic City International Airport.

The strong northwest winds, low humidity levels and unseasonably dry November helped rapidly spread wildfires in New Jersey on the weekend of the 23rd and 24th. The largest wildfire occurred in Old Bridge (Middlesex County) and consumed about 230 acres near the Cheesequake State Park. The fire was first reported about 8 a.m. EST on the 24th east of Dock Road. The fire spread toward the Central Park Development toward Washington Avenue and Michelle Avenue. Several residents were evacuated. Morristown Road and New Jersey State Route 34 were closed. Cheesequake State Park was also closed as a precaution. Over 70 apparatuses were used to fight the fire. The fire was contained at 330 p.m. EST on the 24th and residents were permitted to return. New Jersey State Route 34 was re-opened later that day.||The second largest wildfire occurred in Port Monmouth north of Pew Creek in Monmouth County on the evening of the 23rd. The fire was reported at 735 p.m. EST on the evening of the 23rd. The wildfire burned woods and weeds between William Street, Port Monmouth Road and Bray Avenue. Residents on Port Monmouth Road and William Street were asked to evacuate as their homes were threatened.  Seventy-five firefighters helped battle the blaze. The fire was declared under control at 910 p.m. EST on the 23rd and firefighters left at 11 p.m. EST that evening. The siding of one home was damaged by the heat, but no injuries were reported.||During the early morning of the 24th, in Franklin Township (Gloucester County) on Tuckahoe Road, another wildfire consumed about an acre of forest as well as a two car garage, a shed and a boat. The fire was declared under control in about an hour. No injuries were reported. Other smaller wildfires occurred in East Windsor Township in Mercer County, Edison and Old Bridge in Middlesex County and Middletown and Howell in Monmouth County.||Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and helped rapidly spread the wildfires. November was also the third consecutive month of below average precipitation in New Jersey.

A low pressure system emerging from the Gulf of Mexico combining with a cold air mass in place produced a wintry mix of precipitation at the onset of the event across northwest New Jersey on the 26th. The most protracted wintry precipitation occurred in Sussex County where less than an inch of snow was followed by up to a couple of tenths of an inch of ice in the higher terrain.  South of Sussex County, most of the wintry precipitation was in the form of pockets of freezing rain.  Precipitation started as snow during the morning of the 26th, but transitioned over to sleet and then freezing rain or rain by the afternoon of the 26th.  Temperatures hovered right around the freezing mark, so freezing rain was elevation dependent. Enough warm air moved in during the evening of the 26th to change the precipitation over to plain rain.  Until the changeover, untreated roadways were hazardous. In Sussex County, a tractor-trailer rolled onto its side outside the entrance of High Point State Park on New Jersey State Route 23. The roadway was closed for less than an our until the accident was cleared.  No injuries were reported. ||Snowfall included 0.6 inches in High Point (Sussex County) and 0.3 inches in Wantage (Sussex County).  Ice accumulations included 0.15 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County).  The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure system that emerged from the Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 26th. The low tracked northeastward through the Florida Panhandle on the afternoon of the 26th and into the Carolinas on the evening of the 26th.  Enough cold air was initially in place to cause the wintry mix until the circulation around the low brought in sufficient warm air near the surface from the Atlantic Ocean.

The snow fell at its heaviest during the late afternoon in northwest New Jersey just as it was ending.  ||Representative snowfall included 3.0 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County) and Marcella and Butler in Morris County, 2.5 inches in Wantage (Sussex County) and Knowlton (Warren County) 2.0 inches in Morris Plains (Morris County), Hope (Warren County) and Newton (Sussex County), 1.8 inches in Bound Brook (Somerset County) and Stewartsville (Warren County) 1.5 inches in Whitehouse Station (Hunterdon County), Belle Mead (Somerset County) and Edison Middlesex County), 1.2 inches in Hamilton Township (Mercer County) and 0.7 inches in Florence (Burlington County) and Freehold (Monmouth County).||The Alberta Clipper type low which caused the wintry precipitation moved eastnortheast from the lower Ohio Valley on the evening of the 16th and reached Lake Erie at 7 a.m. EST on the 17th. From there, a secondary low pressure system formed around Philadelphia and moved off the central New Jersey coast during the middle of the afternoon on the 17th. This low became the primary low pressure system as it moved toward Long Island, New York around the time precipitation ended later that afternoon.
Bands of thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding in western parts of Salem and Gloucester Counties during the early evening on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller creeks as well as poor drainage flooding in Gloucester County affecting the municipalities along the Delaware River.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around two inches, most of which fell within one hour. Event precipitation totals included 1.83 inches in East Greenwich Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance combined with a hot and humid air mass to produce strong to locally severe thunderstorms across central and southern New Jersey during the second half of the afternoon and early evening on the 24th. In addition to locally damaging winds, lightning strikes caused damage and there were several reports of hail with the thunderstorms as the freezing level aloft was relatively low in height above the ground. Most of the hail was very small (less than half an inch).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Upper Freehold Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms congealed into a squall line ahead of an approaching cold front during the afternoon. Isolated severe thunderstorms were embedded within the line, mainly over the Northeast NJ Metro area. Heavy rain associated with this line produced flash flooding in Bergen County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a chimney of a house on Finch Road, shorting out circuits and knocking siding off the side of the house. No extensive damage was reported. This was originally reported by a Skywarn Spotter, that was confirmed by a local fire department.

The Passaic River at Two Bridges was above its 9 foot flood stage from 700 p.m. EDT on the 14th through 1015 a.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 9.27 feet at 1230 p.m. EDT on the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most oppressive hot spell of the summer season affected New Jersey from July 15th through the 20th.  Widespread high temperatures reached into the mid to upper 90s and the most oppressive days (combination of heat and humidity) occurred on the 18th and 19th. Morning lows those days were only near 80 degrees in highly urbanized areas in the southwest part of the state. Afternoon heat indices reached 105 to 110 degrees. A large upper level low encompassing the Mississippi Valley resulted in a moisture feed up the east coast from the Gulf of Mexico. A stationary boundary just to the west of New York City was the focus for training showers and thunderstorms that resulted in heavy rain and flash flooding across Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stretch of Rt. 3 near the Garden State Pkwy. in Clifton was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level impulse, coupled with a stationary front just to the west, triggered an isolated severe thunderstorm in Union County that produced a tornado and a microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred along a line approximately 1/4 to 1/2 mile to the right of, and approximately one minute before, the tornado which tracked from near Garfield Road in Berkley Heights to near Evergreen Road in Summit New Jersey. Dozens of trees and power lines were downed along the path of this microburst, including some onto cars and houses. At its widest point, the microburst was about 500 yards wide, and lasted for just under 5 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to locally severe thunderstorms moved into northwest New Jersey during the early evening of the 7th. The thunderstorms were triggered by an approaching upper air disturbance and surface trof.  Thunderstorms that tracked across Morris County dropped hail and produced damaging winds, resulting in downed trees and power lines.  Approximately 5,500 customers in Mine Hill Township, Roxbury Township, and nearby areas lost power as a result of the thunderstorms during the evening of the 7th.  With power outages continuing, Mine Hill and Roxbury Townships opened air-conditioned community centers and town halls for residents who needed to cool down from the ongoing heat and humidity or recharge electronic devices. There were still about 2,500 homes and businesses without power on the afternoon of the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees, power lines, and transmission poles in Dover Township at 8:15 p.m. EDT on the 7th.  Several private residences and homes were damaged as a result.  Tree damage was also reported near St. Clare's Hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped develop a line of strong to locally severe thunderstorms in southern New Jersey during the evening of the 20th. The worst reported wind damage was in Gloucester County. This front also ended the longest and most oppressive hot spell of the summer of 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in East Greenwich Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped develop a line of strong to locally severe thunderstorms in southern New Jersey during the evening of the 20th. The worst reported wind damage was in Gloucester County. This front also ended the longest and most oppressive hot spell of the summer of 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and wires along Hessian Avenue in West Deptford Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front coupled with a deep southerly flow of very moist air caused slow moving and in some instances back building thunderstorms to occur during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. This caused an all-time record breaking rainfall in nearby Philadelphia as well as flash flooding in southwestern New Jersey and parts of the Passaic and Raritan Basins in northern New Jersey.  The flash flooding in the Philadelphia suburbs (Camden County in particular) led to people being stuck on roadways for hours on the 42 Freeway as well as Interstates 295, 76 and 676. Westbound delays on the Atlantic City Expressway reached two hours.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates and or actual measurements reached around 4 inches in parts of northern New Jersey and 4 to 8 inches in parts of southwest New Jersey with the heaviest rain right along the Delaware River. The daily rainfall measurement of 8.02 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport was not only the all-time calendar day record but was estimated to be a one in thousand year event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the torrential downpours that fell overnight caused the Cohansey River at Seeley (Hopewell Township) to crest at its 6 foot flood stage from 715 a.m. EDT through 8 a.m. EDT on the 29th. Event precipitation totals included 2.79 inches in Upper Deerfield Township, 2.57 inches in Greenwich Township and 2.07 inches at Seabrook Farms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of showers and thunderstorms withe heavy rain caused flash flooding in the northwest part of the state during the morning of the 1st. While additional showers and thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon, they were not as heavy. Doppler Radar storm total estimates ranged from 1.5 to 4.5 inches in Somerset, Hunterdon, Warren, Morris and Sussex Counties. The heaviest rain fell in Warren County along the Delaware River. The nearly stationary frontal boundary that has been responsible for heavy rain started to retrograde westward as a Bermuda High pressure system moved west from the Atlantic Ocean.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused small stream and poor drainage flash flooding in Parsippany-Troy Hills Township. Eastbound Interstate 80 was flooded as was Interstate 287 in both directions. The ramp from Interstate 80 to Interstate 287 was also flooded. Ramps onto U.S. Route 46 and Interstate 80 were also flooded and closed. Several streets within the township were covered with rapidly flowing water. East of Morristown, the Columbia Turnpike exit east of New Jersey State Route 24 was flooded and closed. Event precipitation totals included 2.23 inches in Parsippany and 2.19 inches in Morristown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most oppressive hot spell of the summer season affected New Jersey from July 15th through the 20th.  Widespread high temperatures reached into the mid to upper 90s and the most oppressive days (combination of heat and humidity) occurred on the 18th and 19th. Morning lows those days were only near 80 degrees in highly urbanized areas in the southwest part of the state. Afternoon heat indices reached 105 to 110 degrees. To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened or extended the hours of cooling centers.   ||Highest temperatures included 99 degrees in Belmar (Monmouth County), Washington's Crossing (Mercer County) and Berkeley Township (Ocean County), 98 degrees in Hillsborough (Somerset County), Belvidere (Warren County), Somerdale (Camden County), Westampton (Burlington County) and New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 97 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, Cherry Hill (Camden County), Lumberton (Burlington County), Swedesboro (Gloucester County), Andover (Sussex County) and Somerville (Somerset County), 96 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County), Piney Hollow (Gloucester County) and Stewartsville (Warren County), 95 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County) and Parsippany (Morris County), 94 degrees in Woodstown (Salem County) and Kingwood (Hunterdon County) and 93 degrees in Wildwood (Cape May County).| |A stalling cold front brought an end to the excessive heat on the 21st and to 90 degree high temperatures by the 22nd.

A large upper level low encompassing the Mississippi Valley resulted in a moisture feed up the east coast from the Gulf of Mexico. A stationary boundary just to the west of New York City was the focus for training showers and thunderstorms that resulted in heavy rain and flash flooding across Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moving water approximately one to two feet deep inundated many residence properties in Dumont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped develop a line of strong to locally severe thunderstorms in southern New Jersey during the evening of the 20th. The worst reported wind damage was in Gloucester County. This front also ended the longest and most oppressive hot spell of the summer of 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, extending from the Pennsylvania/Maryland border eastward across southern New Jersey, and a series of upper level disturbances resulted in numerous slow-moving thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 22nd through the early morning of the 23rd. These thunderstorms produced torrential rain and some wind damage. Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 7 inches across central to northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of gusty thunderstorm winds and heavy rain brought down several trees along Red Bank Avenue in West Deptford Township at 145 a.m. EDT on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level impulse, coupled with a stationary front just to the west, triggered an isolated severe thunderstorm in Union County that produced a tornado and a microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in New York NY has confirmed a tornado from Berkeley Heights to Summit in Union County New Jersey. The tornado touched down in Berkeley Heights near the Passaic River just west of Garfield Street. It passed northeast through New Providence and lifted in Summit New Jersey near Evergreen Road.||Extensive tree damage was observed along the tornado path with the greatest damage occurring at the sewage treatment plant near Snyder Avenue in Berkeley Heights. Some of the downed trees were upon cars and houses. The National Weather Service also interviewed eyewitnesses of the tornado for additional confirmation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, extending from the Pennsylvania/Maryland border eastward across southern New Jersey, and a series of upper level disturbances resulted in numerous slow-moving thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 22nd through the early morning of the 23rd. These thunderstorms produced torrential rain and some wind damage.||Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 7 inches across central to northern New Jersey.  Run-off from waves of heavy precipitation resulted in areas of poor drainage and roadway flooding, with some creeks and streams also overflowing their banks. In Jackson (Ocean County), rainfall totals of 5 inches were reported in an hour.||In addition, the combination of the heavy rain and higher than normal astronomical tides associated with the full moon, caused minor tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River and upper Delaware Bay during the overnight high tide cycles on the 22nd and lingered into the early morning high tide cycle on the 23rd. In Philadelphia, the highest tide reached 8.76 feet above mean lower low water early on the 23rd. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.  At Reedy Island (New Castle County), the highest tide reached 7.52 feet above mean lower low water late on the 22nd. Minor tidal flooding starts at 7.2 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell the night of the 22nd and again in the early morning hours of the 23rd. The runoff from all of the rain caused the Rancocas Creek to exceed its flood stage. Flooding closed Mill and King Streets as well as Rancocas-Mount Holly Road along the creek. The North Branch of the Rancocas Creek at Iron Works Park was above its 11.7 foot flood stage from 922 a.m. EDT through 145 p.m. EDT on the 23rd. It crested at 11.74 feet at 1115 a.m. EDT.

A slow moving cold front coupled with a deep southerly flow of very moist air caused slow moving and in some instances back building thunderstorms to occur during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. This caused an all-time record breaking rainfall in nearby Philadelphia as well as flash flooding in southwestern New Jersey and parts of the Passaic and Raritan Basins in northern New Jersey.  The flash flooding in the Philadelphia suburbs (Camden County in particular) led to people being stuck on roadways for hours on the 42 Freeway as well as Interstates 295, 76 and 676. Westbound delays on the Atlantic City Expressway reached two hours.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates and or actual measurements reached around 4 inches in parts of northern New Jersey and 4 to 8 inches in parts of southwest New Jersey with the heaviest rain right along the Delaware River. The daily rainfall measurement of 8.02 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport was not only the all-time calendar day record but was estimated to be a one in thousand year event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy downpours caused flash flooding of smaller creeks and roadways in western Cumberland County.  Particularly hard hit by flooding was New Jersey State Route 77 in Upper Deerfield Township and Bridgeton City. Event precipitation totals included 2.79 inches in Upper Deerfield Township, 2.57 inches in Greenwich Township and 2.07 inches at Seabrook Farms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In addition to the flash flooding, an approaching cold front triggered thunderstorms that caused isolated wind damage as well as power outages in New Jersey on the 28th. About 3,000 Atlantic City Electric customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down about eight trees in Pittstown (Alexandria Township).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell through the night of the 22nd and caused urban and poor drainage flash flooding to occur during the early morning of the 23rd. Water estimated to be a foot deep was reported on Hessian Avenue in West Deptford Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that moved through the Raritan River basin and the southern Passaic River basin during the morning of the 1st caused wind damage in Somerset County and southeast Morris County.  Public Service Electric and Gas reported about 8,700 of its customers lost power, with the majority in Somerset County. Jersey Central Power and Gas reported outages in Somerset and Morris Counties. Downed trees and wires caused the suspension of commuter rail service along the Morris-Essex and Gladstone Lines that morning. Train tickets were cross honored on other means of transportation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down power transmission lines along the Morris-Essex New Jersey Commuter Transit Line.

A weakening low pressure system and a dissipating frontal boundary still managed to produce heavy downpours during the second half of the afternoon and first half of the evening across central New Jersey.  This caused poor drainage and roadway flooding. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 1.0 to 2.5 inches in sections of central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and thunderstorms caused heavy rain to fall across the southern half of Mercer County. Roadway flooding was reported in and around Hamilton Township.  Event totals included 1.95 inches in Hamilton Township and 1.60 inches in Lawrence Township. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 1.5 to 2.5 inches in the southern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front coupled with a deep southerly flow of very moist air caused slow moving and in some instances back building thunderstorms to occur during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. This caused an all-time record breaking rainfall in nearby Philadelphia as well as flash flooding in southwestern New Jersey and parts of the Passaic and Raritan Basins in northern New Jersey.  The flash flooding in the Philadelphia suburbs (Camden County in particular) led to people being stuck on roadways for hours on the 42 Freeway as well as Interstates 295, 76 and 676. Westbound delays on the Atlantic City Expressway reached two hours.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates and or actual measurements reached around 4 inches in parts of northern New Jersey and 4 to 8 inches in parts of southwest New Jersey with the heaviest rain right along the Delaware River. The daily rainfall measurement of 8.02 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport was not only the all-time calendar day record but was estimated to be a one in thousand year event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding in western Camden County that damaged vehicles and jammed traffic on major roadways for hours. People were trapped in vehicles for hours. Numerous vehicles were submerged to their tops with multiple rescues on the 42 Freeway, Interstate 76 and in Brooklawn, Camden and Gloucester City. In spite of all of the rescues, no serious injuries were reported. In Gloucester City, U.S. Route 130 was completely submerged. In Collingswood, U.S. Route 30 was closed. On the other side of the Cooper River, waves of water occurred on the Admiral Wilson Boulevard in Pennsauken and Camden. In Barrington, three feet of water accumulated on Clement Bridge Road (New Jersey State Route 41) and raw sewage backed up into apartment buildings. In Camden, the Ferry Avenue PATCO parking lot was flooded. Event precipitation totals included 5.37 inches in Audubon, 5.17 inches in Mount Ephraim, 5.05 inches in Haddonfield, 5.00 inches in Merchantville, 3.63 inches in Collingswood, 3.55 inches in Somerdale, 3.20 inches in Cherry Hill, 2.60 inches in Pennsauken and 2.50 inches in Voorhees Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, extending from the Pennsylvania/Maryland border eastward across southern New Jersey, and a series of upper level disturbances resulted in numerous slow-moving thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 22nd through the early morning of the 23rd. These thunderstorms produced torrential rain and some wind damage.||Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 7 inches across central to northern New Jersey.  Run-off from waves of heavy precipitation resulted in areas of poor drainage and roadway flooding, with some creeks and streams also overflowing their banks. In Jackson (Ocean County), rainfall totals of 5 inches were reported in an hour.||In addition, the combination of the heavy rain and higher than normal astronomical tides associated with the full moon, caused minor tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River and upper Delaware Bay during the overnight high tide cycles on the 22nd and lingered into the early morning high tide cycle on the 23rd. In Philadelphia, the highest tide reached 8.76 feet above mean lower low water early on the 23rd. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.  At Reedy Island (New Castle County), the highest tide reached 7.52 feet above mean lower low water late on the 22nd. Minor tidal flooding starts at 7.2 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused poor drainage and small stream flash flooding in Jackson Township in Ocean County. Several roads were reported to be flooded in Jackson. Event precipitation totals included 6.07 inches in Jackson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most oppressive hot spell of the summer season affected New Jersey from July 15th through the 20th.  Widespread high temperatures reached into the mid to upper 90s and the most oppressive days (combination of heat and humidity) occurred on the 18th and 19th. Morning lows those days were only near 80 degrees in highly urbanized areas in the southwest part of the state. Afternoon heat indices reached 105 to 110 degrees. To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened or extended the hours of cooling centers.   ||Highest temperatures included 99 degrees in Belmar (Monmouth County), Washington's Crossing (Mercer County) and Berkeley Township (Ocean County), 98 degrees in Hillsborough (Somerset County), Belvidere (Warren County), Somerdale (Camden County), Westampton (Burlington County) and New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 97 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, Cherry Hill (Camden County), Lumberton (Burlington County), Swedesboro (Gloucester County), Andover (Sussex County) and Somerville (Somerset County), 96 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County), Piney Hollow (Gloucester County) and Stewartsville (Warren County), 95 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County) and Parsippany (Morris County), 94 degrees in Woodstown (Salem County) and Kingwood (Hunterdon County) and 93 degrees in Wildwood (Cape May County).| |A stalling cold front brought an end to the excessive heat on the 21st and to 90 degree high temperatures by the 22nd.

Scattered strong to locally severe thunderstorms moved into northwest New Jersey during the early evening of the 7th. The thunderstorms were triggered by an approaching upper air disturbance and surface trof.  Thunderstorms that tracked across Morris County dropped hail and produced damaging winds, resulting in downed trees and power lines.  Approximately 5,500 customers in Mine Hill Township, Roxbury Township, and nearby areas lost power as a result of the thunderstorms during the evening of the 7th.  With power outages continuing, Mine Hill and Roxbury Townships opened air-conditioned community centers and town halls for residents who needed to cool down from the ongoing heat and humidity or recharge electronic devices. There were still about 2,500 homes and businesses without power on the afternoon of the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell from a thunderstorm in Ledgewood (Roxbury Township).

A large upper level low encompassing the Mississippi Valley resulted in a moisture feed up the east coast from the Gulf of Mexico. A stationary boundary just to the west of New York City was the focus for training showers and thunderstorms that resulted in heavy rain and flash flooding across Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hollywood Ave. in Ho-Ho-Kus was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, extending from the Pennsylvania/Maryland border eastward across southern New Jersey, and a series of upper level disturbances resulted in numerous slow-moving thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 22nd through the early morning of the 23rd. These thunderstorms produced torrential rain and some wind damage. Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 7 inches across central to northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief gustnado formed along a gust front from a severe thunderstorm at 510 p.m. EDT on the 22nd in the Cedar Glen West community along the Manchester and Jackson Township border in Ocean County. A gust front is the leading edge of winds that are produced from a downdraft in a severe thunderstorm. It is along this gust front that some rotation developed and a gustnado formed. A gustnado's rotation begins at the ground and spins up, and it does not connect with any cloud base rotation. The gustnado on the afternoon of the 22nd contained winds of up to 60 mph, and it brought down several trees and produced some minor roof damage in the Cedar Glen West community.  Two homes along Vath Street suffered minor roof damage.

Heavy rain that fell on the 10th caused additional flooding across the central third of New Jersey. Event precipitation totals averaged between 1.0 and 2.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain that fell during the 10th led to flooding of gaged rivers in Somerset County from the evening of the 10th through the early afternoon of the 12th. The Millstone River at Griggstown was above its 10 foot flood stage from 856 p.m. EDT on the 10th through 805 p.m. EDT on the 12th. It crested at 12.76 feet at 430 p.m. EDT on the 11th. The Millstone River at Millstone was above its 8.5 foot flood stage from 552 a.m. EDT on the 11th through 122 p.m. EDT on the 12th. It crested at 9.51 feet at 800 p.m. EDT on the 11th. The North Branch Raritan River at South Branch was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1040 p.m. EDT on the 10th through 508 a.m. EDT on the 11th. It crested at 7.67 feet at 230 a.m. EDT on the 11th. Event precipitation totals included 1.80 inches Hillsborough Township, 1.75 inches in Franklin Township, 1.67 inches in Warren Township, 1.62 inches in Montgomery Township, and 1.51 inches in Bridgewater Township.

The intersection of Rancocas Road and the Mount Holly By-Pass (County Route 541) was flooded and closed. The basement of the First Presbyterian Church also flooded. In Mount Laurel, flash flooding along Parkers Creek shut down Hartford and Union Mill Rods. Event precipitation totals included 6.71 inches in Eastampton, 6.25 inches at the Mount Holly WFO, 4.00 inches in Florence, 3.43 inches in Hainesport and 3.36 inches in Mount Laurel.

Event precipitation totals averaged 1 to 2.5 inches in Mercer, Salem and Cumberland Counties as well as the northern half of Cape May County and 2.5 to 5.5 inches in the southern half of Cape May County.

Event precipitation totals included 5.60 inches in Green Creek, 5.51 inches in Erma, 3.83 inches in Cape May,  3.71 inches in Cape May Harbor and 3.12 inches in Wildwood Crest.

The remnants of Tropical Storm Andrea moving up the Eastern Seaboard, from the Southeast States to the Mid Atlantic and Northeast Coasts, led to waves of heavy rain and thunderstorms from the early morning hours through the late evening on the 7th. The initial burst of heavy precipitation arrived during the early morning on the 7th as moisture surged into the Mid Atlantic Region ahead of Tropical Storm Andrea, with the center of its low pressure located near the coast of Georgia. Run-off from waves of heavy precipitation resulted in areas of poor drainage and flash flooding, which caused some smaller rivers across southern New Jersey to overflow their banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding along roadways and small creeks in central Salem County. Of note, roadway flooding occurred along NJ Route 45 north of County Route 660 (Point Airy Road) near milemarker 12 in Pilesgrove Township. All lanes were closed, and traffic was detoured around this stretch of NJ Route 45 due to flooding. Doppler radar storm total estimates ranged between 3 to 4 inches of rainfall through central Salem County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that fell on the 10th caused additional flooding across the central third of New Jersey. Event precipitation totals averaged between 1.0 and 2.5 inches.

The Cooper River at Haddonfield was above its 2.8 foot flood stage from 1252 p.m. EDT through 451 p.m. EDT on the 10th, and it crested at 2.99 feet at 215 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 1.91 inches in Mount Ephraim, 1.88 inches in Collingswood, 1.69 inches in Merchantville, and 1.42 inches in Somerdale.

Event precipitation totals averaged 1 to 2.5 inches in Mercer, Salem and Cumberland Counties as well as the northern half of Cape May County and 2.5 to 5.5 inches in the southern half of Cape May County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding in West Windsor Township. Several roads were flooded including the right lane flooding closure of County Route 571 within the township. Event precipitation totals included 1.82 inches in Lawrence Township and 1.72 inches in Ewing Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that fell on the 10th caused additional flooding across the central third of New Jersey. Event precipitation totals averaged between 1.0 and 2.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front coupled with a deep southerly flow of very moist air caused slow moving and in some instances back building thunderstorms to occur during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. This caused an all-time record breaking rainfall in nearby Philadelphia as well as flash flooding in southwestern New Jersey and parts of the Passaic and Raritan Basins in northern New Jersey.  The flash flooding in the Philadelphia suburbs (Camden County in particular) led to people being stuck on roadways for hours on the 42 Freeway as well as Interstates 295, 76 and 676. Westbound delays on the Atlantic City Expressway reached two hours.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates and or actual measurements reached around 4 inches in parts of northern New Jersey and 4 to 8 inches in parts of southwest New Jersey with the heaviest rain right along the Delaware River. The daily rainfall measurement of 8.02 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport was not only the all-time calendar day record but was estimated to be a one in thousand year event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding in western Salem County and affected Carneys Point and Pennsville Township the hardest. In Pennsville, two women were rescued from their vehicles on North Broadway. In Carneys Point, Ives Avenue, Hickory Avenue and U.S. Route 130 were all flooded and closed. A Cumberland Farms store within the township suffered flood damage. Field flash flooding damaged tomato crops in the county. Event precipitation totals included 5.93 inches in Pennsville Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused small stream and poor drainage flash flooding in western Burlington County. The Southwest Branch of the Rancocas Creek flooded sections of the Golden Pheasant Golf Course in Lumberton. In Southampton. several apartment buildings flooded at the Sherwood Village Complex. Event precipitation totals included 2.91 inches in Mount Laurel, 2.76 inches in Mount Holly, 2.37 inches in Cinnaminson, 2.32 inches in Westampton, 2.08 inches in Palmyra and 2.05 inches in Moorestown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that fell on the 10th caused additional flooding across the central third of New Jersey. Event precipitation totals averaged between 1.0 and 2.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain through the morning to afternoon of the 10th led to flooding of some roadways in and around Wall Township. Flooding caused lane closures on the Garden State Parkway between mile-markers 91 and 93 and also between mile-markers 95 and 97.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of showers and thunderstorms withe heavy rain caused flash flooding in the northwest part of the state during the morning of the 1st. While additional showers and thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon, they were not as heavy. Doppler Radar storm total estimates ranged from 1.5 to 4.5 inches in Somerset, Hunterdon, Warren, Morris and Sussex Counties. The heaviest rain fell in Warren County along the Delaware River. The nearly stationary frontal boundary that has been responsible for heavy rain started to retrograde westward as a Bermuda High pressure system moved west from the Atlantic Ocean.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused small stream and poor drainage flash flooding as well as field flooding in the northwest part of Somerset County centered in and around Bridgewater, Branchburg, Bedminster and Bernards Townships. The North Branch area was flooded in Branchburg Township. Burnt Mills Road was flooded and closed as was New Jersey State Route 28 (Easton Turnpike) at the North Branch Bridge. In Bridgewater Township, flooding along U.S. Route 22 blocked the eastbound entrance to Interstate 287. A few flooded roadways had rapidly moving water passing over them in Bernards Township. This included left lane flooding on Interstate 287 that necessitated a closure.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around three inches in the township and street flooding was reported. Event precipitation totals included 2.34 inches in Bernards Township and 2.29 inches in Basking Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that moved through the Raritan River basin and the southern Passaic River basin during the morning of the 1st caused wind damage in Somerset County and southeast Morris County.  Public Service Electric and Gas reported about 8,700 of its customers lost power, with the majority in Somerset County. Jersey Central Power and Gas reported outages in Somerset and Morris Counties. Downed trees and wires caused the suspension of commuter rail service along the Morris-Essex and Gladstone Lines that morning. Train tickets were cross honored on other means of transportation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in Hillsborough Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms formed along a surface trough extending west of New York City as a weak upper level shortwave moved through the Northeast in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and wires were down around the town of Caldwell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on car on Merritt Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of strong to isolated severe thunderstorms formed along the sea breeze front in Ocean County during the early evening on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped quarter size hail in Barnegat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalling cold front helped trigger strong thunderstorms during the second half of the afternoon on the 21st in the southern half of New Jersey.  Also the combination, of the full moon and some of the highest astronomical tides of 2013, caused minor tidal flooding to occur along the New Jersey shore during the evening high tide cycle from the 21st through the 25th. The highest tides (all during the evening high tide cycle on the 22nd) included 6.43 feet above mean lower low water in Atlantic City (Atlantic County), 7.36 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County) and 7.32 feet above mean lower low water in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.0 feet above mean lower low water in Atlantic City and 6.7 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May and Sandy Hook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike took down a couple of wires in Bordentown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped develop a line of strong to locally severe thunderstorms in southern New Jersey during the evening of the 20th. The worst reported wind damage was in Gloucester County. This front also ended the longest and most oppressive hot spell of the summer of 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and wires in Woodbury.  A Skywarn spotter measured a wind gust of 52 mph withiin the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most oppressive hot spell of the summer season affected New Jersey from July 15th through the 20th.  Widespread high temperatures reached into the mid to upper 90s and the most oppressive days (combination of heat and humidity) occurred on the 18th and 19th. Morning lows those days were only near 80 degrees in highly urbanized areas in the southwest part of the state. Afternoon heat indices reached 105 to 110 degrees. To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened or extended the hours of cooling centers.   ||Highest temperatures included 99 degrees in Belmar (Monmouth County), Washington's Crossing (Mercer County) and Berkeley Township (Ocean County), 98 degrees in Hillsborough (Somerset County), Belvidere (Warren County), Somerdale (Camden County), Westampton (Burlington County) and New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 97 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, Cherry Hill (Camden County), Lumberton (Burlington County), Swedesboro (Gloucester County), Andover (Sussex County) and Somerville (Somerset County), 96 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County), Piney Hollow (Gloucester County) and Stewartsville (Warren County), 95 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County) and Parsippany (Morris County), 94 degrees in Woodstown (Salem County) and Kingwood (Hunterdon County) and 93 degrees in Wildwood (Cape May County).| |A stalling cold front brought an end to the excessive heat on the 21st and to 90 degree high temperatures by the 22nd.

The remnants of Tropical Storm Andrea moving up the Eastern Seaboard, from the Southeast States to the Mid Atlantic and Northeast Coasts, led to waves of heavy rain and thunderstorms from the early morning hours through the late evening on the 7th. The initial burst of heavy precipitation arrived during the early morning on the 7th as moisture surged into the Mid Atlantic Region ahead of Tropical Storm Andrea, with the center of its low pressure located near the coast of Georgia. Through the course of the day, the cyclone became post-tropical as it accelerated northeastward along the East Coast of the United States, delivering another round of heavy rain with some strong thunderstorms to the Mid Atlantic States during the late afternoon and evening of the 7th. ||Doppler radar storm total estimates ranged from around 2 to 5 inches of rain for the event across New Jersey.  Run-off from waves of heavy precipitation resulted in areas of poor drainage and flash flooding, which caused some smaller rivers across southern New Jersey to overflow their banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to flooding of smaller rivers and streams in Somerset County during the evening of the 7th.  The North Branch Raritan River at South Branch was above its 7 foot flood stage from 731 p.m. EDT on the 7th through 110 p.m. EDT on the 8th. It crested at 9.61 feet at 645 a.m. EDT on the 8th. The Pike Run at Belle Mead was above its 7 foot flood stage from 915 p.m. EDT on the 7th through 418 a.m. EDT on the 8th. It crested at 7.80 feet at 100 a.m. EDT on the 8th. The Lamington River at Burnt Mills was above its 9.5 foot flood stage from 933 p.m. EDT on the 7th through 435 a.m. EDT on the 8th. It crested at 9.94 feet at 200 a.m. on the 8th. The Green Brook at Plainfield was above its 8 foot flood stage from 1041 p.m. EDT on the 7th through 206 a.m. EDT on the 8th. It crested at 8.50 feet at 1245 a.m. EDT on the 8th. ||Then, runoff from heavy rain that fell from the 7th into the 8th led to flooding on several gaged rivers and streams across Somerset County. The Raritan River at Bound Brook was above its 28 foot flood stage from 748 a.m. EDT through 745 p.m. EDT on the 8th, and it crested at 29.14 feet at 130 p.m. EDT. The Raritan River at Manville was above its 14 foot flood stage from 624 a.m. EDT through 451 p.m. EDT on the 8th, and it crested at 15.24 feet at 1215 p.m. EDT. The Millstone River at Blackwells Mills reported moderate flooding and was above its 9 foot flood stage from 948 a.m. EDT on the 8th through 522 p.m. EDT on the 9th. It crested at 12.62 feet at 130 p.m. EDT on the 8th. The Millstone River at Griggstown reported major flooding and was above its 10 foot flood stage from 819 p.m. EDT on the 7th through 630 a.m. EDT on the 10th. It crested at 16.16 feet at 830 a.m. EDT on the 8th. The Millstone River at Millstone was above its 8.5 foot flood stage from 128 a.m. EDT on the 8th through 241 a.m. EDT on the 10th. It crested at 13.25 feet at 345 p.m. EDT on the 8th. The Millstone River at Weston was above its 12.4 foot flood stage from 114 p.m. EDT through 325 p.m. EDT on the 8th. It crested at 12.44 feet at 230 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 4.42 inches in Blackwells Mills, 4.26 inches in Montgomery, 4.08 inches in Bridgewater Township, 4.07 inches in Branchburg, 4.03 inches in Hillsborough Township, 3.97 inches in Basking Ridge, and 3.82 inches at the Somerville Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that moved through the Raritan River basin and the southern Passaic River basin during the morning of the 1st caused wind damage in Somerset County and southeast Morris County.  Public Service Electric and Gas reported about 8,700 of its customers lost power, with the majority in Somerset County. Jersey Central Power and Gas reported outages in Somerset and Morris Counties. Downed trees and wires caused the suspension of commuter rail service along the Morris-Essex and Gladstone Lines that morning. Train tickets were cross honored on other means of transportation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several large trees, tree limbs and wires in Bridgewater Township. There were a few road closures. Downed trees and wires were reported on Old York as well as Foothill Road.

The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, extending from the Pennsylvania/Maryland border eastward across southern New Jersey, and a series of upper level disturbances resulted in numerous slow-moving thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 22nd through the early morning of the 23rd. These thunderstorms produced torrential rain and some wind damage. Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 7 inches across central to northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning from early morning thunderstorms struck a house in Island Heights, Ocean County at 505 a.m. EDT on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front coupled with a deep southerly flow of very moist air caused slow moving and in some instances back building thunderstorms to occur during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. This caused an all-time record breaking rainfall in nearby Philadelphia as well as flash flooding in southwestern New Jersey and parts of the Passaic and Raritan Basins in northern New Jersey.  The flash flooding in the Philadelphia suburbs (Camden County in particular) led to people being stuck on roadways for hours on the 42 Freeway as well as Interstates 295, 76 and 676. Westbound delays on the Atlantic City Expressway reached two hours.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates and or actual measurements reached around 4 inches in parts of northern New Jersey and 4 to 8 inches in parts of southwest New Jersey with the heaviest rain right along the Delaware River. The daily rainfall measurement of 8.02 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport was not only the all-time calendar day record but was estimated to be a one in thousand year event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the torrential downpours that fell earlier on the day on the 28th caused minor to moderate flooding on area creeks and rivers in northern Camden County.  The Cooper River at Haddonfield had moderate flooding and was above its 2.8 foot flood stage from 625 p.m. EDT on the 28th through 526 a.m. EDT on the 29th. It crested at 4.13 feet at 1115 p.m. EDT on the 28th.  The South Branch of the Pennsauken Creek at Cherry Hill was above its 9 foot flood stage from 1022 p.m. EDT on the 28th through 639 a.m. EDT on the 29th. It crested at 10.03 feet at 3 a.m. EDT on the 29th. Event precipitation totals included 3.20 inches in Cherry Hill, 2.60 inches in Pennsauken and 2.50 inches in Voorhees Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure remained nearly stationary to the south resulting in excessive heat across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of high heat and humidity resulted in a heat index of 108 degrees for a few afternoon hours at Newark International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of showers and thunderstorms withe heavy rain caused flash flooding in the northwest part of the state during the morning of the 1st. While additional showers and thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon, they were not as heavy. Doppler Radar storm total estimates ranged from 1.5 to 4.5 inches in Somerset, Hunterdon, Warren, Morris and Sussex Counties. The heaviest rain fell in Warren County along the Delaware River. The nearly stationary frontal boundary that has been responsible for heavy rain started to retrograde westward as a Bermuda High pressure system moved west from the Atlantic Ocean.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused small stream and poor drainage flash flooding in central Hunterdon County from Kingwood and Alexandria Townships northeastward through Readington and Tewksbury Townships. U.S. Route 22 in Readington Township was flooded in both directions as was Interstate 78 in Clinton Township. In Readington Township, the approach to the Rockefellow Mills Bridge over the South Branch of the Raritan River was flooded. Flooding was reported along the South Branch of the Rockaway Creek in Whitehouse Station, the Neshanic River at Reaville (crested at 6.94 feet), the South Branch of the Raritan River near Stanton and the Mulhockaway Creek in Clinton. The Beaver Brook flooded into the parking lot of the municipal building in Clinton.  Widespread street flooding was reported in Kingwood Township. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around 3 inches in this band and actual storm totals included 2.50 inches in Readington Township, 2.41 inches in Franklin Township, 2.40 inches in Lebanon Township, 2.34 inches in Frenchtown, 2.08 inches in Stockton, 1.95 inches in Pittstown and 1.71 inches in Kingwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly moving cold front and a wave of low pressure triggered a large area of light to moderate rain over New Jersey on the 18th.  But, embedded within the area were strong to locally severe thunderstorms that occurred during the afternoon mainly across the central third of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees and wires in Upper Freehold Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meteotsunami believed to have been caused by the passing of a weak end derecho off the New Jersey coast occurred during the afternoon of the 13th. An ocean wave associated with it injured a father and his son at Barnegat Inlet (Ocean County). The tidal departure caused by the meteotsunami ranged from three inches to one foot along the New Jersey coast, but some rough surf and higher waves occurred at the time of its occurrence.||The meteotsunami was believed induced by a weak end derecho that moved off the New Jersey coast at about 11 a.m. EDT on the morning of the 13th. The surface pressure fluctuation caused by this phenomenon was believed to have triggered it. The worst reported incident with the meteotsunami occurred in Ocean County at Barnegat Inlet at around 330 p.m. EDT on the 13th. A man was spear fishing near the mouth of Barnegat Inlet, just south of a submerged northern breakwater.  The outgoing tide (high tide was at about Noon EDT) was amplified by strong currents which carried divers over the submerged breakwater (normally 3-4 feet deep). This strong outrush continued for about two minutes and eventually the rocks in the submerged breakwater were exposed.  At this point, the eyewitness noticed a large wave coming in, approximately six feet peak-to-trough and spanned across the inlet. The upper two feet of the wave was breaking. This wave occurred in conjunction with a reversal of the current such that even though the tide was receding, a strong surge was entering the inlet. This surge carried the divers back over the submerged reef and into the inlet from where they were rescued. On the south jetty, three people were swept off the rocks which were 5 to 6 feet above sea level at the time into the ocean on the beach side of the jetty.  A father and son were injured and required medical treatment.  Both made it to shore on their own. The father suffered rib injuries and a punctured lung and was flown by helicopter to Jersey South Hospital. The 10-year-old son suffered a cut leg and cheek and was taken by ambulance  to Southern Ocean Medical Center.  The ambulance the boy was in, was involved in a traffic accident on the way to the hospital. The driver was injured, but the boy was not hurt further in the crash. There was no more strong wave activity after about five minutes. ||Other meteotsunamis have occurred along the east coast in Boothbay Harbor. Maine in 2008 and in Daytona Beach, Florida in 1992.
