EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a high pressure system over the area and a very humid air mass caused dense fog to form in southwest New Jersey during the morning of the 21st.  Visibilities were reduced to less than one-quarter of a mile at times. The dense fog contributed to a traffic accident in which  a man was seriously injured after his vehicle struck and killed a cow on Salem-Hancock Bridge Road in Lower Alloways Creek Township in Salem County. After striking the cow, the vehicle veered off the road into a yard, ran over a dog house (no dog was inside), side-swiped a house's an air conditioning unit and stopped inches away from the house itself.  A medical evacuation helicopter could not be used because of the dense fog and the man was transported by ambulance to Newark, Delaware. The dense fog dispersed by 830 a.m. EDT that morning.

A complex of showers and thunderstorms produced both wind damage and flash flooding across southern new Jersey. The wind damage included an EF0 tornado in Manahawkin (Stafford Township in Ocean County). An isolated severe thunderstorm then occurred during the evening of the 13th in Morris County.  Cloud-to-ground lightning strikes peaked at 6,000 per hour as this complex moved through New Jersey. The thunderstorms caused about 14,500 homes and businesses to lose power on the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down in Manahawkin (Stafford Township) on the morning of the 13th.  The estimated maximum wind speed was 85 mph and the maximum path width was 100 yards. The estimated path length was 3.2 miles. There were no reported injuries or fatalities and damage was estimated at $250,000.  The tornado initially touched down in the Cedar Run section near the intersection of U.S. Route 9 and Oak Avenue. The tornado lifted while it was moving through the Manahawkin Wildlife Management area. Numerous trees were blown down or snapped along the path of this tornado as were utility poles. There was roof and second floor wind damage to the Lighthouse Christian Academy and also the Manahawkin Baptist Church off of Hilliard Boulevard. One downed tree damaged a home, a trailer was flipped and wind damage occurred to the Moose Lodge off of New Jersey State Route 72. The worst reported wind damage occurred around Oak Avenue, East Bay Avenue and Hilliard Boulevard.  U.S. Route 9, New Jersey State Route 72, Hilliard Boulevard, Beach Avenue, Stafford Avenue and Parker Street were all closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trof preceding a cold front (that moved through overnight on thee 22nd) helped trigger a series of showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain across the central third of New Jersey from the morning into the middle of the afternoon on the 22nd.  Event precipitation totals reached 1.5 to 3.5 inches across sections of Mercer, Middlesex, Morris and Somerset Counties and even reached close to 6.0 inches in far northwest Somerset County which had the greatest impact. The heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller streams, roadways and rivers and also led to flooding along the North Branch of the Raritan River that persisted into the evening of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding of roadways and streams in northwestern Somerset County and hit Bedminister, Peapack-Gladstone and Bernardsville the hardest. The flash flooding affected New Jersey Commuter Rail Service. Eastbound commuter rail service along the Morris and Essex Line was suspended for the day after flash flooding caused four to six washouts of stone supports along the Gladstone Branch tracks. Westbound service continued, but delays averaged 45 minutes.  Substitute eastbound bus service was provided. About ten people in Peapack-Gladstone self evacuated to the Municipal Building. The runoff from the heavy rain and also caused flooding along the Lamington River and Middle Brook.  Event precipitation totals included 5.85 inches in Peapack-Gladstone, 5.14 inches in Pottersville, 4.94 inches in Far Hills and 3.99 inches in Middlebush.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the heavy rain earlier in the day caused flooding to persist along sections of the Middle Brook, the Lamington River and the North Branch of the Raritan River.  The Lamington River at Burnt Mills was above its 9.5 foot flood stage from 147 p.m. EDT through 815 p.m. EDT on the 22nd. It crested at 12.00 feet at 5 p.m. EDT. It was its highest crest since 12.1 feet on April 15, 2007.  The Middle Brook at Burnt Mills was above its 8 foot flood stage from 1131 a.m. EDT through 428 p.m. EDT on the 22nd. It crested at 9.75 feet at 115 p.m. EDT.  The North Branch of the Raritan River at North Branch was above its 12.3 foot flood stage from 519 p.m. EDT through 933 p.m. EDT on the 22nd. It crested at 13.51 feet at 730 p.m. EDT.  Farther downstream, the North Branch of the Raritan River at the village of South Branch was above its 7 foot flood stage from 937 p.m. EDT through 1055 p.m. EDT on the 22nd. It crested at 7.07 feet at 1045 p.m. EDT.   Event precipitation totals included 5.85 inches in Peapack-Gladstone, 5.14 inches in Pottersville, 4.94 inches in Far Hills, 3.99 inches in Middlebush, 3.52 inches in Hillsborough, 3.50 inches in Manville, 3.02 inches in Bridgewater, 2.90 inches in Franklin and 1.88 inches in Montgomery Township.

High temperatures were about the same both days, but the 7th was slightly more humid. Morning low temperatures were in the 70s, with some upper 70s near Philadelphia.  The passage of a weak trof dropped temperatures slightly on the 8th.||To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers.  ||Highest temperatures included 95 degrees in Howell (Monmouth County) and Point Pleasant (Ocean County), 94 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, West Creek and Berkeley Township (Ocean County), Hamilton (Mercer County), Somerville (Somerset County) and Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 93 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County), Oswego Lake (Burlington County), Piney Hollow (Gloucester County), Cherry Hill (Camden County), Belmar and Sea Girt (Monmouth County), Morristown (Morris County), Walpack (Sussex County) and Belvidere (Warren County), 92 degrees in High Bridge (Hunterdon County), Millville (Cumberland County), Hammonton (Atlantic County), Cape May Courthouse (Cape May County)  and 91 degrees in Woodstown (Salem County).

A lee side trof preceding a cold front (that moved through overnight on thee 22nd) helped trigger a series of showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain across the central third of New Jersey from the morning into the middle of the afternoon on the 22nd.  Event precipitation totals reached 1.5 to 3.5 inches across sections of Mercer, Middlesex, Morris and Somerset Counties and even reached close to 6.0 inches in far northwest Somerset County which had the greatest impact. The heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller streams, roadways and rivers and also led to flooding along the North Branch of the Raritan River that persisted into the evening of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flash flooding of roadways and smaller streams in central and northern Middlesex County affecting North Brunswick and East Brunswick the hardest. In East Brunswick, numerous roadways were flooded (including New Jersey State Routes 18 and 27) and traffic was described as at a standstill. In New Brunswick, the basement of the Middlesex County Family Court was flooded and Family Court was closed for the rest of the day.  Joyce Kilmer Avenue, George Street, Johnson Drive and Livingston Avenue were all flooded. Buccleuch Park was closed. In Edison, New Jersey State Route 27 was flooded. Street flooding was also reported in Woodbridge. Event precipitation totals included 2.00 inches in Cranbury and 1.90 inches in New Brunswick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of showers and thunderstorms produced both wind damage and flash flooding in central and southwest New Jersey on the 13th. Doppler Radar storm total estimates averaged 2.5 to 5.0 inches. In addition to widespread poor drainage flooding, flooding also affected the Raccoon Creek in Gloucester County, the Cooper River in Camden County and the Great Egg River in Atlantic County. The flooding caused the suspension of Amtrak Northeast Corridor train service and sixty minute delays on the North Jersey Coastal Commuter Rail Service on the 13th. There was system-wide cross honoring of commuter tickets. Sections of major roadways that were flooded and closed included Interstate 76 and New Jersey State Routes 35, 42 and 55. Numerous vehicles were trapped in flood waters and rescues were performed. But, no serious injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding of roadways and creeks, especially across the central third of Burlington County. In Evesham Township, a school bus became stuck in flood waters on Erindale Drive. Thirty-one children were rescued. Also within the township, a motorist was rescued from flood waters on New Jersey State Route 70 near Cropwell Road. Flooded and closed roadways also included Marlton Parkway in Evesham and Hartford Road in Medford. The flooding also affected the primary election as voting booths had to be moved after flooding occurred at some polling places. Event precipitation totals included 2.64 inches in Medford Township, 2.53 inches in Southampton Township, 2.35 inches in Pemberton Township and 2.30 inches in Tabernacle Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding of roadways and creeks, especially in the eastern half of Monmouth County.  Sections of New Jersey State Routes 35 and 71 were flooded and closed.  Roadway closures occurred in Ocean Township, Long Branch, Manasquan, Deal, Wall and Neptune. Several dozen vehicles became struck in flood waters. In Long Branch, a mother and her nine-month-old son were rescued from a vehicle on Long Branch Avenue. Three other roadways were flooded. In Ocean Township, secondary roadways took a big flooding hit. In Manasquan, basement flooding occurred along Marcellus Avenue. Event precipitation totals included 4.11 inches in Ocean Township, 2.85 inches in Fair Haven, 2.68 inches in Red Bank, 2.57 inches in Rumson and 2.16 inches in Long Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough over the eastern United States and a frontal boundary stationary to the south of Long Island were the triggers for shower and thunderstorm development across the region.  Some of these storms produced heavy rainfall, which resulted in flash flooding in Passaic County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 23 northbound was closed at High Crest Drive in Smiths Mills due to flooding.

A strong cold front produced a convective line of showers and scattered thunderstorms with damaging winds that moved through New Jersey during the morning of the 1st. About 3,000 homes and businesses lost power, mainly in the northern half of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in Harding Township.  Downed trees and wires blocked Baileys Mill Road between Lees Hill and Young Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong south to southwest winds preceding a cold frontal passage brought an unseasonably mild and moist air mass into New Jersey. This caused heavy rain to fall and caused poor drainage flooding as well as isolated stream and river flooding in northwest New Jersey on the 31st.  Most of the waterway flooding was minor and all streams and  rivers were back within bankfull by the evening of the 31st. Event precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 2.5 inches in northwest New Jersey.  The rain began during the late afternoon on the 30th and fell at its heaviest during the early morning of the 31st as convective elements moved through New Jersey.  The rain ended around 7 a.m. EST on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Ditch at Beaver Brook Road was above its 2.5 foot flood stage from 537 a.m. EST through 107 p.m. EST on the 31st. It crested at 2.84 feet at 625 a.m. EST. Event precipitation totals included 1.96 inches in Mount Olive, 1.93 inches in Washington Township, 1.61 inches in Denville, 1.59 inches in Rockaway and 1.56 inches in Boonton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a slowly moving cold front with a wave of low pressure along it and wet antecedent conditions helped develop a large area of light to moderate rain with embedded thunderstorms accompanied by heavy rain in New Jersey primarily during the afternoon of the 18th. This led to urban and poor drainage flooding and also flash flooding near and along some of the smaller creeks and rivers in the state. Event precipitation totals averaged 1 to 3 inches in the southern half of the state and in sections of Morris County in the northern part of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding in Surf City on Long Beach Island. Vehicles were disabled by the flood waters on Central and Barnegat Avenues and Long Beach Boulevard. Nearby event precipitation totals included 1.90 inches in Harvey Cedars and 1.88 inches in Seaside Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced a convective line of showers and scattered thunderstorms with damaging winds that moved through New Jersey during the morning of the 1st. About 3,000 homes and businesses lost power, mainly in the northern half of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in Pittstown (Franklin Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front triggered a line of showers and isolated thunderstorms produced widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph and isolated stronger wind damage in New Jersey during the early morning of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A convective line knocked down large tree limbs and power lines along Creek Road in Mount Laurel Township. The roadway was closed into the afternoon of the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, extending from the Pennsylvania/Maryland border eastward across southern New Jersey, and a series of upper level disturbances resulted in numerous slow-moving thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 22nd through the early morning of the 23rd. These thunderstorms produced torrential rain and some wind damage.||Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 7 inches across central to northern New Jersey.  Run-off from waves of heavy precipitation resulted in areas of poor drainage and roadway flooding, with some creeks and streams also overflowing their banks. In Jackson (Ocean County), rainfall totals of 5 inches were reported in an hour.||In addition, the combination of the heavy rain and higher than normal astronomical tides associated with the full moon, caused minor tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River and upper Delaware Bay during the overnight high tide cycles on the 22nd and lingered into the early morning high tide cycle on the 23rd. In Philadelphia, the highest tide reached 8.76 feet above mean lower low water early on the 23rd. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.  At Reedy Island (New Castle County), the highest tide reached 7.52 feet above mean lower low water late on the 22nd. Minor tidal flooding starts at 7.2 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell through the night of the 22nd and caused urban and poor drainage flooding to occur during the early morning of the 23rd. The Admiral Wilson Boulevard in Camden was closed due to flooding with vehicles reported to be stuck in high waters. Route 130 in Pennsauken near Browning Road was closed due to significant flooding. Event precipitation totals included 1.97 inches in Merchantville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a slowly moving cold front with a wave of low pressure along it and wet antecedent conditions helped develop a large area of light to moderate rain with embedded thunderstorms accompanied by heavy rain in New Jersey primarily during the afternoon of the 18th. This led to urban and poor drainage flooding and also flash flooding near and along some of the smaller creeks and rivers in the state. Event precipitation totals averaged 1 to 3 inches in the southern half of the state and in sections of Morris County in the northern part of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the heavier rain earlier in the day caused minor flooding along the Cooper River.  The Cooper River at Haddonfield was above its 2.8 foot flood stage from 608 p.m. EDT through 1030 p.m. EDT on the 18th. It crested at 3.01 feet at 830 p.m. EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of showers and thunderstorms produced both wind damage and flash flooding across southern new Jersey. The wind damage included an EF0 tornado in Manahawkin (Stafford Township in Ocean County). An isolated severe thunderstorm then occurred during the evening of the 13th in Morris County.  Cloud-to-ground lightning strikes peaked at 6,000 per hour as this complex moved through New Jersey. The thunderstorms caused about 14,500 homes and businesses to lose power on the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in Franklin Township. Tree wind damage was concentrated along County Route 538 from Coles Mill to White Hall Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough over the eastern United States and a frontal boundary stationary to the south of Long Island were the triggers for shower and thunderstorm development across the region.  Some of these storms produced heavy rainfall, which resulted in flash flooding in Passaic County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 46 was closed in both directions east of Piaget Ave. in Clifton. due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front triggered a line of severe thunderstorms that moved through Northeast New Jersey. The line also spawned a tornado in Bergen County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a house along Tiffany Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning low end derecho moved through the region on the 13th which caused strong to severe thunderstorms to develop across southern New Jersey. The derecho moved off the New Jersey coast by late morning leaving a boundary across parts of the region. Later in the day, a strong low pressure system brought a cold front through the region, which sparked off more strong to severe thunderstorms across the area. Atlantic City Electric reported approximately 1774 outages in the region due to the storms.||In addition to wind, heavy rain was reported with these storms. In general, 1 to 2 inches of rain fell across the state with the heaviest amounts reported across the north central and the southern parts of the state. Rainfall amounts included 2.03 inches in Greenwich Township (Cumberland County), 1.92 inches in Holmdel (Monmouth County), 1.75 inches in Bethlehem Township (Hunterdon County), 1.72 inches in Upper Deerfield Township (Cumberland County), 1.71 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 1.69 inches in Vineland (Cumberland County), 1.67 inches in Andover (Sussex County) and Holland Township (Hunterdon County), 1.62 in Estell Manor (Atlantic County) and Pittsgrove Township (Salem County), 1.60 inches in Edison (Middlesex County) and Franklin Township (Somerset County), 1.59 inches in Bridgewater Township (Somerset County) and Blairstown (Warren County), 1.52 inches in Chatham (Morris County), 1.51 inches in Cranbury (Middlesex County), 1.48 inches in Folsom (Atlantic County) and Woodbine (Cape May County), and 1.45 inches in Washington Township (Warren County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Entrance to Cape May County Park on Route 9 had several large tree limbs down, which took down power lines.
